qa_pairs.question: How do I reset my password for an Alliance national cluster?
qa_pairs.answer: You can reset your password for any Alliance national cluster by visiting https://ccdb.alliancecan.ca/security/forgot.

qa_pairs.question: When can I reset my password for an Alliance national cluster?
qa_pairs.answer: You will not be able to reset your password until your first role gets approved by Alliance staff.

qa_pairs.question: Why can't I use Ctrl+C to copy text in the Linux terminal?
qa_pairs.answer: In the Linux terminal, Ctrl+C means "Cancel" or "Interrupt" and stops the running program, which is why it cannot be used to copy text.

qa_pairs.question: How do I copy text in a Linux terminal?
qa_pairs.answer: In most cases under Windows and Linux, you can use Ctrl+Insert to copy text. Depending on your terminal software, simply selecting the text might also copy it into the clipboard.

qa_pairs.question: How do I paste text in a Linux terminal?
qa_pairs.answer: In most cases under Windows and Linux, you can use Shift+Insert to paste text. Depending on your terminal software, you can also paste from the clipboard by using either the right-click or middle-click.

qa_pairs.question: What are the copy and paste commands for macOS users in the terminal?
qa_pairs.answer: Users of macOS can continue to use Cmd+C to copy and Cmd+V to paste in the terminal.

qa_pairs.question: Why do text files prepared in Windows sometimes cause problems in a Linux environment?
qa_pairs.answer: Text files prepared in a Windows environment have an additional invisible "carriage return" character at the end of each line, which differs from the convention used by Linux and OS X, causing problems when reading these files in a Linux environment.

qa_pairs.question: How can I resolve issues with text file line endings when working between Windows and Linux?
qa_pairs.answer: You should either create and edit your text files directly on the cluster using standard Linux text editors like Emacs, Vim, and Nano, or if you prefer Windows, use the `dos2unix <filename>` command on the cluster login node to convert the file's line endings.

qa_pairs.question: Why does saving files in Emacs sometimes take a long time on shared filesystems?
qa_pairs.answer: Emacs uses the `fsync` system call when saving files to reduce the risk of losing data during a system crash. This extra reliability can cause saves to take several seconds, even for small files, when writing to a shared filesystem (e.g., `home`, `scratch`, `project`) on a cluster.

qa_pairs.question: How can I improve the performance of saving files in Emacs if it is slow?
qa_pairs.answer: To increase Emacs saving performance, add the line `(setq write-region-inhibit-fsync t)` to your `~/.emacs` file.

qa_pairs.question: Where can I find more information about customizing save settings in Emacs?
qa_pairs.answer: More information about this Emacs setting can be found on the GNU Emacs manual at https://www.gnu.org/savannah-checkouts/gnu/emacs/manual/html_node/emacs/Customize-Save.html.

qa_pairs.question: What does the error message "sbatch: error: Batch job submission failed: Socket timed out on send/recv operation" indicate?
qa_pairs.answer: This message typically appears when the load on the Slurm manager or scheduler process is too high.

qa_pairs.question: What should I do if I encounter the "sbatch: error: Batch job submission failed: Socket timed out on send/recv operation" error?
qa_pairs.answer: If you receive this error, wait a minute or so, then run `squeue -u $USER` to check if your job was accepted. If it doesn't appear, simply submit it again.

qa_pairs.question: How can I determine why my jobs are in the pending (PD) state?
qa_pairs.answer: You can determine why your jobs are in the pending (PD) state by running the `squeue -u <username>` command on the cluster, and checking the `(REASON)` column.

qa_pairs.question: What do the `Resources` and `Priority` reasons signify for pending jobs?
qa_pairs.answer: `Resources` means the cluster is very busy, and you might need to wait or submit a job requesting fewer resources. `Priority` means your job has a lower priority because you or your research group have been over-consuming cluster resources recently.

qa_pairs.question: How can I monitor my research group's consumption of cluster resources?
qa_pairs.answer: You can monitor your research group's over- or under-consumption of cluster resources using the `sshare` command.

qa_pairs.question: What does the `LevelFS` column in `sshare` output mean?
qa_pairs.answer: The `LevelFS` column indicates your over- or under-consumption of cluster resources: a value greater than one means under-consumption (consuming fewer than your fair share), while a value less than one means over-consumption (consuming more).

qa_pairs.question: How does over-consumption of resources impact job priority?
qa_pairs.answer: The more you over-consume resources, meaning the closer the `LevelFS` value gets to zero, the more your pending jobs will decrease in priority.

qa_pairs.question: Is the `LevelFS` value consistent across all clusters?
qa_pairs.answer: No, the `LevelFS` value is unique to the specific cluster you are using.

qa_pairs.question: Does the scheduler permanently remember past resource consumption for priority calculations?
qa_pairs.answer: No, there is a memory effect to this calculation, so the scheduler gradually "forgets" about any potential over- or under-consumption of resources from months past.
